topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the locker room felt thick, heavy with the scent of sweat and old leather. Liam smoothed down his jersey for the tenth time, the fabric sticking uncomfortably to his skin. His hands, usually steady on the field, were trembling slightly as he stared at the roster posted on the wall. His name wasn't there. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, a sensation that made his breath catch in his throat. He tried to focus on the rhythmic squeak of sneakers from the adjacent court, but all he could register was the silence where his acceptance should have been. When Coach Miller finally called the names, Liam stood up, his legs feeling strangely disconnected from his body. He managed a tight, forced smile as he walked toward the exit, the sound of his footsteps echoing too loudly in the cavernous room.

[story 2]

"Just breathe, Liam. You’ve trained harder than anyone," his teammate, Sarah, whispered, placing a reassuring hand on his shoulder. But the words barely registered over the frantic drumming in his chest. He kept glancing at the team photo on the bulletin board, the smiling faces radiating an effortless camaraderie he felt utterly excluded from. Every time he looked at the designated spots for the starters, a wave of dizzying uncertainty washed over him. He fiddled incessantly with the drawstring of his shorts, his jaw tight. When the coach finally announced the final selections, Liam felt a sudden, sharp intake of air, followed by a strange hollowness. He nodded, a jerky, almost involuntary motion, and quickly turned to leave before anyone could ask him a question.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the gymnasium seemed to burn with an intensity that mirrored the churning in Maya’s gut. She clutched the strap of her duffel bag so tightly her knuckles were white. She had visualized this moment a thousand times—the roar of the crowd, the feeling of the team uniform settling onto her shoulders. Now, standing just outside the main entrance, the noise of the pre-game warm-ups sounded like a distant, mocking wave. Her palms were slick. She shifted her weight from one foot to the other, a restless energy buzzing beneath her skin. When the coach called the final group, Maya felt a strange lightness, as if the pressure had suddenly evaporated, leaving behind only a vast, echoing emptiness. She managed a polite nod and walked toward the exit, her gaze fixed firmly on the polished floor tiles.
